title: Cross Region Image Viewer using VPC Peering

steps.text: Click on the Open Console button, and you will get redirected to AWS Console in a new browser tab.
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. Otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your Username and Password in the lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. Otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your Username and Password in the lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Once Signed in to the AWS Management Console, Make the default AWS Region as US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1.
In this milestone, you will create two Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) in different AWS regions. These VPCs will later be connected using VPC Peering for secure, cross-region communication.
Region: Virginia (us-east-1) : FrontendVPC (hosts EC2 web app)
Region: Mumbai (ap-south-1) : BackendVPC (hosts S3 bucket + S3 endpoint)
This VPC will be used to host the frontend EC2 instance (web server).
Navigate to the VPC Dashboard in the AWS Console.
Make sure you are in region to N. Virginia (us-east-1).
Click Create VPC and then Select VPC Only.
You have now created the Frontend VPC in Virginia.
In this step, you will configure the FrontendVPC so that the EC2 instance can be accessed publicly over the Internet
Navigate to VPC Dashboard, Click on Subnets in the left side of the Panel
You have created a public subnet inside FrontendVPC.
Go to VPC Dashboard, Select Internet Gateways in the left side of the panel
Select the newly created IGW, Select Attach to VPC, then choose FrontendVPC.
Internet Gateway is now attached to FrontendVPC.
Go to VPC Dashboard, Select Route Tables in the left side of the panel
Select the route table, go to Routes, Click on Edit routes , then Add route:
Go to Subnet Associations, Click on Edit subnet associations.
The public subnet now routes Internet-bound traffic via FrontendIGW.
Open the VPC Dashboard in the AWS Console.
Click Create VPC, Select VPC Only.
Enable DNS Hostnames and DNS Resolution
You have created the BackendVPC in Mumbai.
Go to VPC Dashboard, Click Subnets
Navigate to VPC Dashboard, Click Route Tables from the left side of the panel
Select the new route table, Click go to Subnet Associations and choose Edit subnet associations.
Backend private subnet is now associated with its route table.
To allow the EC2 in Virginia to fetch images from S3 in Mumbai without Internet, you will use a Gateway VPC Endpoint.
Go to VPC Dashboard, Choose Endpoints and click Create Endpoint.
Make sure you're in Mumbai Region.
Your S3 bucket is now accessible privately within the VPC (no Internet required).

In this milestone, you will create a private S3 bucket in the Mumbai region (ap-south-1) and upload test images. This bucket will act as the central storage for images, which will later be accessed securely from the Virginia frontend via VPC Peering.
Go to the S3 Dashboard in the AWS Console.
In Block Public Access settings, keep all options checked (we will use private access only).
In the Bucket Versioning, Check Enabled.
You have successfully created a private S3 bucket in Mumbai.
Before setting up cross-region access, upload some sample images to test the setup.
In the S3 Console, navigate to the bucket: cross-region-image-store.
Select one or more image files (.jpg or .png)
You can upload these photos of these cars for example, porsche.jpg, ferrari.jpg, mclaren.jpg
Keep all permissions private (no need to modify).
Test images are now stored securely in S3. These will later be accessed by the Virginia EC2 frontend app through private networking.
It’s time to connect these two VPCs using VPC Peering. This will enable secure, private communication between both regions without using the public internet.
Make sure you’re in Virginia region (us-east-1), open the VPC Dashboard.
Go to Peering Connections, Click Create Peering Connection.
VPC ID (Accepter): Give BackendVPC VPC ID (Mumbai)
A peering request has been sent from Virginia to Mumbai.
Go to VPC Dashboard, Choose Peering Connections.
You will see the pending request. Select it and Click Actions then choose Accept Request.
The VPC peering connection is now active between Virginia and Mumbai.
For communication to work, you must update the route tables in both regions to direct traffic through the peering connection.
Under Routes, Select Edit routes and click on Add route:
Under Routes, Select Edit routes and click on Add route:
Both VPCs can now communicate privately over the peering connection.
Finally, update the S3 bucket policy so that only traffic coming from the Virginia VPC via peering is allowed.
Go to S3 Console, Click on Your Bucket
Select Permissions and click on Bucket Policy.
Paste the following policy (replace <Your-Bucket-Name>, <Virginia-VPC-ID> and <Your-Account-ID> with actual values):
Now, only the Virginia VPC can securely access the private S3 bucket in Mumbai.
This EC2 instance will act as the frontend application server. It will run a Streamlit app that fetches and displays images stored in the private S3 bucket (located in Mumbai).
Make sure you’re in Virginia region. Go to EC2 Dashboard
Key Pair (login): Use an existing key pair (or create a new one, e.g. image-key).
In Security Group, Give name as Image-Project-SG
Custom TCP (8501) from Anywhere (for Streamlit UI)
IAM Role: Attach the IAM Role of cross_region_project_<RANDOM-NUMBERS>
NOTE: Replace <Public-IP-of-Frontend-EC2> with the Public IPv4 address from the EC2 console.
If you see the Streamlit sample app, everything’s ready!
Run these commands inside EC2 after connecting:
Paste the following code into the editor:
Press Ctrl + X then Y and then Click Enter
At this point, your frontend EC2 in Virginia can securely fetch and display private images stored in Mumbai S3 using cross-region VPC peering.
In this milestone, you will test the Streamlit app running on the Virginia EC2 instance to ensure it can display private images stored in the Mumbai S3 bucket.
Open your browser and go to:
Note: Replace <Your-EC2-Public-IP> with the public IP address of the Virginia EC2 instance.
The Cross-Region Image Viewer application will open.
In the dropdown menu labeled “Choose me” option, click to expand.
You will see a list of objects (images) that you uploaded earlier to the Mumbai S3 bucket (cross-region-image-store).
Select any image from the list and click on Enter.
The selected image will be fetched securely across regions (from Mumbai to Virginia) and displayed in the application interface.
If you can see your images, the cross-region architecture is working correctly.
All communication happens privately via VPC peering, without making the S3 bucket public.
Once the project steps are completed, please click on the Validation button on the right-side panel.
This will validate the resources in the AWS account and displays whether you have completed this project successfully or not.
You have successfully created two isolated VPCs in Mumbai and Virginia , established Inter-Region VPC Peering for private connectivity.
You have successfully configured an S3 Gateway Endpoint in Mumbai to enable secure, private access to the S3 bucket.
You have successfully created a private S3 bucket in Mumbai, uploaded test images, and applied strict bucket policies and IAM roles.
You have successfully launched an EC2 instance in Virginia, installed dependencies, and deployed a Streamlit image viewer app.
You have successfully ensured that all data transfer between regions happens privately over AWS’s backbone network, without using the public internet.
You have successfully simulated a real-world multi-region SaaS architecture, showcasing network security, cost optimization, and resilience against ransomware-style attacks.
You have successfully completed the Project.
Once you have completed the steps, click on End Project from the lab console.
